docs.title: Python Package Tools in 30 mins
docs.text:
TL;DR: A comprehensive guide to Python package managers and virtual environment tools, from built-in solutions like pip and venv to modern alternatives like Poetry and uv.

  • Python's ecosystem offers numerous tools for managing packages and virtual environments
  • Choosing the right tool depends on:
  • Project requirements
  • Need for simple dependency management
  • Cross-language support requirements
  • Performance needs
  • This guide organizes tools by category to help select the best fit for your workflow

docs.title: pip
docs.text:
• What it does: Default Python package installer that downloads and installs packages from PyPI (Python Package Index)
  • Installs packages from PyPI

  • Works with requirements.txt for dependency specifications 

    # Install a single package from PyPI
> pip install requests

# Install all packages listed in requirements.txt (common for project setup)
> pip install -r requirements.txt

# Export all installed packages with their versions to requirements.txt (for sharing/reproducing environment)
> pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Install a specific version of a package (for version pinning)
> pip install requests==2.28.0

# Upgrade a package to the latest version
> pip install --upgrade requests

# Uninstall a package
> pip uninstall requests

  • Best for: Basic package installation and simple projects

docs.title: venv
docs.text:
• What it does: Creates isolated Python environments to separate project dependencies and avoid conflicts between projects
  • Built-in virtual environment tool (Python 3.3+)

  • Creates isolated environments with their own Python interpreter and packages 

    # Create a new virtual environment named 'venv' in the current directory
> python -m venv venv

# Activate the virtual environment on macOS/Linux (makes Python and pip point to the venv)
> source venv/bin/activate

# Activate on Windows
> venv\Scripts\activate.bat

# Deactivate the current virtual environment (returns to system Python)
> deactivate

# Create a venv without pip (if you want to install pip separately)
> python -m venv venv --without-pip

# Remove a virtual environment (just delete the directory)
> rm -rf venv

  • Best for: Basic environment isolation without extra dependencies

docs.title: virtualenv
docs.text:
• What it does: Creates isolated Python environments like venv, but with more features and flexibility
  • Older alternative to venv but still maintained and enhanced
  • More features including Python version discovery and environment templates

  • Slightly faster environment creation 

    # Install virtualenv globally (required before first use)
> pip install virtualenv

# Create a new virtual environment named 'myenv'
> virtualenv myenv

# Create a virtualenv with a specific Python version (if multiple Pythons are installed)
> virtualenv -p python3.11 myenv

# Create a virtualenv using system site-packages (inherits globally installed packages)
> virtualenv --system-site-packages myenv

# Activate the virtualenv (same as venv)
> source myenv/bin/activate

# List available Python interpreters that virtualenv can use
> virtualenv --discovery cached

  • Best for: Advanced virtual environment needs or pre-Python 3.3 projects

docs.title: pipenv
docs.text:
• What it does: Unifies package management and virtual environment creation into a single tool, automatically managing both for you
  • Combines pip + venv functionality
  • Uses Pipfile and Pipfile.lock for deterministic dependency resolution

  • Automatic virtual environment management 

    # Install a package and automatically create/update Pipfile and Pipfile.lock
> pipenv install requests

# Activate a subshell within the virtual environment (creates venv if it doesn't exist)
> pipenv shell

# Install dev dependencies (e.g., testing tools, linters)
> pipenv install --dev pytest

# Install all dependencies from Pipfile.lock (for reproducible deployments)
> pipenv sync

# Update all packages to their latest compatible versions
> pipenv update

# Run a command within the virtual environment without activating shell
> pipenv run python script.py

  • Best for: Simple project workflows requiring both dependency and environment management

docs.title: Poetry
docs.text:
• What it does: Comprehensive tool for dependency management, packaging, and publishing Python projects using modern standards
  • Modern dependency management with sophisticated resolution
  • Uses pyproject.toml (PEP 518 standard)
  • Built-in dependency resolution and lockfile generation

  • Handles packaging and publishing to PyPI 

    # Initialize a new Poetry project interactively (creates pyproject.toml)
> poetry init

# Add a package as a dependency (updates pyproject.toml and poetry.lock)
> poetry add requests

# Activate a shell within the virtual environment
> poetry shell

# Add a dev dependency (e.g., testing or linting tools)
> poetry add --group dev pytest

# Install all dependencies from poetry.lock (for setting up project on new machine)
> poetry install

# Build distribution packages (wheel and sdist) for publishing
> poetry build

# Publish package to PyPI
> poetry publish

# Update dependencies to their latest compatible versions
> poetry update

# Run a command within the virtual environment without activating shell
> poetry run python script.py

  • Best for: Modern Python applications and libraries requiring robust dependency management and publishing

docs.title: Conda
docs.text:
• What it does: Cross-language package and environment manager that handles both Python packages and system dependencies (C libraries, compilers, etc.)
  • Cross-language package + environment manager
  • Popular in data science and scientific computing communities
  • Manages Python versions and binary dependencies

  • Installs pre-compiled binaries for faster installation 

    # Create a new environment with a specific Python version
> conda create -n myenv python=3.11

# Activate an environment (makes it the active Python)
> conda activate myenv

# Install a package from conda channels (pre-compiled binaries)
> conda install numpy

# Install multiple packages at once (recommended for better dependency resolution)
> conda install numpy pandas matplotlib

# Export environment to a file (for sharing/reproducing)
> conda env export > environment.yml

# Create environment from a YAML file
> conda env create -f environment.yml

# Deactivate current environment (returns to base)
> conda deactivate

# List all environments
> conda env list

# Remove an environment
> conda env remove -n myenv

  • Best for: Data science and scientific computing projects requiring complex binary dependencies

docs.title: Mamba
docs.text:
• What it does: Drop-in replacement for Conda with a faster C++ dependency solver, providing the same functionality with significantly better performance
  • Faster alternative to conda
  • Compatible with conda commands and packages
  • Faster dependency solver (written in C++)

  • Particularly beneficial for large, complex environments 

    # Create a new environment (same as conda but much faster)
> mamba create -n myenv python=3.11

# Activate environment (use conda activate, not mamba activate)
> conda activate myenv

# Install packages with faster dependency resolution than conda
> mamba install numpy pandas scikit-learn

# Update packages (significantly faster than conda update)
> mamba update --all

# Search for available packages
> mamba search tensorflow

# Install from a YAML file (faster than conda)
> mamba env create -f environment.yml

  • Best for: Large scientific environments where conda's solver is too slow

docs.title: uv
docs.text:
• What it does: Ultra-fast Rust-based package installer and resolver that serves as a drop-in replacement for pip with 10-100x faster performance
  • Extremely fast Rust-based package manager
  • Drop-in pip replacement with compatible CLI
  • Can manage virtual environments

  • Supports lockfiles for reproducible installs 

    # Create a virtual environment (10-100x faster than venv)
> uv venv

# Install packages (significantly faster than pip)
> uv pip install requests

# Install from requirements.txt (with parallel downloads)
> uv pip install -r requirements.txt

# Compile dependencies to a lockfile (for reproducible installs)
> uv pip compile requirements.in -o requirements.txt

# Sync environment to exactly match lockfile (removes unlisted packages)
> uv pip sync requirements.txt

# Install a specific version with fast resolution
> uv pip install "django>=4.0,<5.0"

  • Best for: Fast modern workflows where performance matters, especially for CI/CD pipelines

docs.title: pipx
docs.text:
• What it does: Installs and runs Python CLI applications in isolated environments, making them globally available without dependency conflicts
  • Installs Python CLI tools globally in isolated environments
  • Each tool gets its own virtual environment
  • Tools are globally accessible as commands

  • Prevents dependency conflicts between CLI tools 

    # Install a CLI tool globally in its own isolated environment
> pipx install black

# Install another tool (gets separate venv, no dependency conflicts)
> pipx install pytest

# Run a tool temporarily without installing it (downloads, runs, then cleans up)
> pipx run cowsay "Hello!"

# Upgrade an installed tool to the latest version
> pipx upgrade black

# List all installed tools and their versions
> pipx list

# Uninstall a tool and its isolated environment
> pipx uninstall black

# Upgrade all installed tools at once
> pipx upgrade-all

  • Best for: Installing and managing CLI tools like black, pytest, or cookiecutter globally

docs.title: Comparison Overview
docs.text: Tool Manages Packages Manages Venv Lockfile Python Version Mgmt Best For pip Yes No No No Basic installs venv No Yes No No Environment isolation only virtualenv No Yes No No Legacy / advanced isolation pipenv Yes Yes Yes No Simple project workflows poetry Yes Yes Yes No Modern apps and libraries conda Yes Yes No Yes Data science mamba Yes Yes No Yes Large scientific environments uv Yes Yes Yes No Fast modern workflows pipx Yes Auto No No Installing CLI tools

docs.title: Ripgrep in 30 mins
docs.text: TL;DR ripgrep is a blazingly fast search tool that combines the usability and speed.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: • In this tutorial, we'll explore why ripgrep is my search tool and how to use it effectively

docs.title: Why Ripgrep?
docs.text:
• ripgrep stands out for several compelling reasons:
  • Speed: Written in Rust with highly optimized algorithms, ripgrep is often 5-10x faster than alternatives
  • Smart defaults: Automatically respects .gitignore, skips binary files, and excludes hidden directories
  • Parallel execution: Searches multiple files simultaneously using all available CPU cores
  • Memory efficient: Uses memory-mapped files and streaming search to handle large files gracefully
  • Cross-platform: Works seamlessly on Linux, macOS, and Windows

  • Rich features: Supports regex, multiline search, file type filtering, and context display

  • Here's a speed comparison on a typical codebase:

Tool Time (seconds) Notes grep -r 4.2 Basic recursive search ack 3.5 Perl-based search rg 0.4 Ripgrep with default settings

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
• Installing ripgrep is straightforward across all platforms

  • On macOS using Homebrew: 

    > brew install ripgrep

  • On Ubuntu/Debian: 

    > apt-get install ripgrep

  • Verify the installation: 

    > rg --version

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
• The simplest ripgrep command searches for a pattern in the current directory: 
    > rg "pattern"

  • This recursively searches all files, respecting .gitignore and skipping binary files automatically

  • Search with case-insensitive matching: 

    > rg -i "pattern"

  • Search in a specific directory: 

    > rg "pattern" /path/to/directory

  • Search only in files matching a glob pattern: 

    > rg "pattern" -g "*.py"

  • Show line numbers (enabled by default when output is to terminal): 

    > rg -n "pattern"

docs.title: Understanding the Output
docs.text:
• ripgrep output is designed for clarity: 
    > rg "def calculate"
    Output: 
    src/utils.py
23:def calculate_total(items):
45:def calculate_average(values):

src/models.py
12:    def calculate_score(self):
    The format is:
  • Filename (colored for visibility)
  • Line number followed by colon
  • The matching line with pattern highlighted

docs.title: File Type Filtering
docs.text:
• ripgrep understands common file types: 

    > rg "pattern" -t py
    This searches only Python files

  • List all available types: 

    > rg --type-list

  • Exclude specific file types: 

    > rg "pattern" -T js

docs.title: Context Lines
docs.text:
• Show lines before and after matches: 

    > rg "pattern" -C 3

  • Show only lines before: 

    > rg "pattern" -B 2

  • Show only lines after: 

    > rg "pattern" -A 2

docs.title: Search Only Filenames
docs.text:
• List files containing matches without showing the matches: 

    > rg "pattern" -l

  • List files not containing matches: 

    > rg "pattern" --files-without-match

docs.title: Multiline Search
docs.text:
• Search across multiple lines: 
    > rg -U "pattern1.*pattern2"
    The -U flag enables multiline mode where . matches newlines

docs.title: Word Boundaries
docs.text:
• Match whole words only: 
    > rg -w "word"
    This prevents matching "word" inside "password" or "wording"

docs.title: Find All TODO Comments
docs.text:
• Find all TODO comments: 
    > rg "TODO|FIXME|XXX" -t py

docs.title: Search for Function Definitions
docs.text:
• Search for function definitions: 
    > rg "^def \w+\(" -t py

docs.title: Find All Imports of a Module
docs.text:
• Find all imports of a module: 
    > rg "^import pandas|^from pandas" -t py

docs.title: Search in Git History
docs.text:
• Combine with git to search across all branches: 

    > git grep "pattern" $(git rev-list --all)

  • But for current working tree, ripgrep is faster: 

    > rg "pattern"

docs.title: Find Large Files with Pattern
docs.text:
• Find large files with pattern: 
    > rg "pattern" --stats
    The --stats flag shows per-file statistics including file sizes searched

docs.title: Configuration and Customization
docs.text:
• Create a configuration file at ~/.ripgreprc: 

    # Always show line numbers
--line-number

# Always show context
--context=2

# Custom colors
--colors=match:fg:red
--colors=match:style:bold

  • Enable the config file: 

    > export RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH=~/.ripgreprc

docs.title: Ignore Additional Patterns
docs.text:
• Create a .rgignore file in your project root: 
    # Ignore build artifacts
build/
dist/
*.pyc

# Ignore large data files
data/
*.csv

docs.title: Search Hidden Files
docs.text:
• By default, ripgrep skips hidden files
  • Include them: 
    > rg "pattern" --hidden

docs.title: Search All Files Including Ignored
docs.text:
• Override .gitignore and search everything: 
    > rg "pattern" --no-ignore

docs.title: Replace Text Across Files
docs.text:
• While ripgrep doesn't replace text, combine it with sed: 
    > rg "old_pattern" -l | xargs sed -i 's/old_pattern/new_pattern/g'

docs.title: Count Matches
docs.text:
• Count occurrences of a pattern: 

    > rg "pattern" -c

  • Show total count across all files: 

    > rg "pattern" -c | awk -F: '{sum+=$2} END {print sum}'

docs.title: Pattern Escaping
docs.text:
• Special regex characters need escaping: 
    > rg "function\(\)"  # Match "function()"
> rg '\$variable'     # Match "$variable"

docs.title: Binary Files
docs.text:
• ripgrep skips binary files by default
  • To search them: 
    > rg "pattern" -a

docs.title: Symbolic Links
docs.text:
• By default, ripgrep doesn't follow symbolic links
  • To follow them: 
    > rg "pattern" -L

docs.title: Vim Integration
docs.text:
• Add to .vimrc: 

    set grepprg=rg\ --vimgrep
set grepformat=%f:%l:%c:%m

  • Use with: 

    :grep pattern
:copen

docs.title: VS Code Integration
docs.text:
• VS Code uses ripgrep by default for file searching
  • Configure search exclusions in settings.json: 
    {
  "search.exclude": {
    "**/node_modules": true,
    "**/dist": true
  }
}

docs.title: Command Line Aliases
docs.text:
• Add useful aliases to your shell configuration: 
    # Search Python files
alias rgpy='rg -t py'

# Search with context
alias rgc='rg -C 3'

# Search and open in vim
rgv() { vim $(rg -l "$1"); }

docs.title: Comparison with Other Tools
docs.text:
Ripgrep vs grep:

Feature ripgrep grep Speed +++ + Respects .gitignore Yes No Parallel search Yes Limited Automatic binary exclusion Yes No Regex flavor Rust POSIX

Ripgrep vs The Silver Searcher:

Feature ripgrep ag Speed +++ ++ Memory usage Lower Higher Unicode support Better Good Active development Yes Limited

docs.title: UV in 30 mins
docs.text: TL;DR uv is a blazingly fast Python package manager written in Rust that replaces pip, pip-tools, and virtualenv with a single tool that is 10-100x faster.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• This tutorial explores why uv is becoming the go-to package manager for Python developers and how to use it effectively

  • Python's packaging ecosystem challenges:

  • Long criticized for complexity

  • Slow performance

  • uv addresses these pain points:

  • Unified solution
  • Fast performance
  • Modern dependency management
  • Efficient virtual environment handling

docs.title: Why UV?
docs.text:
• Speed: Written in Rust with highly optimized algorithms, uv is 10-100x faster than pip for dependency resolution and installation
  • All-in-one tool: Replaces multiple tools (pip, pip-tools, virtualenv, pyenv) with a single unified interface
  • Drop-in replacement: Compatible with pip and requirements.txt format, making migration seamless
  • Better dependency resolution: Uses a modern SAT solver for more reliable dependency resolution
  • Cross-platform: Works seamlessly on Linux, macOS, and Windows
  • Disk space efficient: Smart caching reduces redundant downloads and storage
  • Project management: Built-in support for creating and managing Python projects

Here's a speed comparison for installing packages:

Tool Time (seconds) Notes pip install 45.2 Standard installation poetry install 38.7 With lock file uv pip install 2.1 First install with caching uv pip install 0.3 Subsequent install (cached)

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
• On macOS and Linux using the official installer:
> curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
  • Using Homebrew on macOS:
> brew install uv
  • Using pip (ironically):
> pip install uv

docs.title: Verifying installation
docs.text:
• Verify the installation: 
    > uv --version
uv 0.9.5 (Homebrew 2025-10-21

docs.title: Creating a Virtual Environment
docs.text:
• Create a virtual environment: 
    > uv venv
Using CPython 3.14.2 interpreter at: /opt/homebrew/opt/python@3.14/bin/python3.14
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Activate with: source .venv/bin/activate

  • This creates a .venv directory with a fresh Python environment 

    > ls .venv/
bin          CACHEDIR.TAG lib          pyvenv.cfg

  • Activate the environment: 

    > source .venv/bin/activate
> which python
/Users/USER/.venv/bin/python

  • Create a virtual environment with a specific Python version: 

    > uv venv --python 3.9 --clear; source .venv/bin/activate; python --version
Using CPython 3.9.6 interpreter at: /Library/Developer/CommandLineTools/usr/bin/python3
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Activate with: source .venv/bin/activate
Python 3.9.6

docs.title: Installing Packages
docs.text:
• Install a package using uv pip: 

    > uv venv --python 3.14 --clear; source .venv/bin/activate
Using CPython 3.14.2 interpreter at: /opt/homebrew/opt/python@3.14/bin/python3.14
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Activate with: source .venv/bin/activate

> which python; python --version
Python 3.14.2

> python -c "import requests; print(requests.__version__)"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    import requests; print(requests.__version__)
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'

> uv pip install requests
Resolved 5 packages in 86ms
Installed 5 packages in 14ms
 + certifi==2026.1.4
 + charset-normalizer==3.4.4
 + idna==3.11
 + requests==2.32.5
 + urllib3==2.6.3

> python -c "import requests"

> python -c "import requests; print(requests.__version__)"
2.32.5

  • Install multiple packages: 

    > uv pip install requests pandas numpy

  • Install from a requirements file: 

    > more requirements.txt
requests
pandas
numpy

> uv pip install -r requirements.txt

  • Install with specific version constraints: 

    > uv pip install "django>=4.0,<5.0"

  • Install from a requirements file with pinned versions: 

    > more requirements.txt
requests==2.31.0
pandas==2.2.0
numpy==1.26.4

> uv pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Initializing a New Project
docs.text:
• Create a new project with scaffolding:
> uv init myproject
> cd myproject
  • This creates a new project with:
    • pyproject.toml: Project configuration and dependencies
    • README.md: Project documentation
    • src/myproject/: Source code directory
    • .python-version: Python version specification

docs.title: Dev vs Prod Dependencies
docs.text:
• Production Dependencies = required to run your application in production

  • In pyproject.toml: 

    [project]
dependencies = [
  "fastapi"
]

  • Development Dependencies = needed only for development (testing, linting, formatting)

  • Not required in production
  • Example: pytest
  • In pyproject.toml: 
    [project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
  "pytest"
]

docs.title: Working with pyproject.toml
docs.text:
• Example pyproject.toml configuration:
[project]
name = "myproject"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "requests>=2.31.0",
    "pandas>=2.0.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=7.0.0",
    "black>=23.0.0",
]
  • Add a dependency to your project:
> uv add requests

  • Add a development dependency: 

    > uv add --dev pytest

  • Remove a dependency: 

    > uv remove requests

docs.title: Syncing Dependencies
docs.text:
• Sync your environment with project dependencies:
> uv sync
  • This installs all dependencies specified in pyproject.toml and creates or updates the lock file

docs.title: Creating Lock Files
docs.text:
• Generate a lock file for reproducible builds:
> uv lock

  • This creates uv.lock with exact versions of all dependencies and their transitive dependencies

  • Install from lock file:

> uv sync --frozen

  • The --frozen flag ensures no changes are made to the lock file

  • Update dependencies to latest compatible versions:

> uv lock --upgrade
  • Update a specific package:
> uv lock --upgrade-package requests

docs.title: Python Version Management
docs.text:
• List available Python versions:
> uv python list
  • Install a specific Python version:
> uv python install 3.11
  • Use a specific Python version for a project:
> uv python pin 3.11
  • This creates a .python-version file in your project

docs.title: Running Commands
docs.text:
• Run Python scripts without activating the environment:
> uv run python script.py
  • Run a command with dependencies:
> uv run --with requests python -c "import requests; print(requests.__version__)"
  • This temporarily installs requests and runs the command

docs.title: Tool Management
docs.text:
• Install and run Python tools globally:
> uv tool install black
  • Run a tool without installing:
> uvx black .

  • The uvx command (or uv tool run) is similar to npx for Node.js

  • List installed tools:

> uv tool list

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
• Show cache information:
> uv cache dir
  • Clean the cache:
> uv cache clean
  • Clean cache for a specific package:
> uv cache clean requests

docs.title: Migrating from pip
docs.text:
• If you have an existing project with requirements.txt:
> uv venv
> source .venv/bin/activate
> uv pip install -r requirements.txt
  • Generate a lock file from requirements:
> uv pip compile requirements.in -o requirements.txt

docs.title: Creating a requirements.txt
docs.text:
• Freeze current environment to requirements:
> uv pip freeze > requirements.txt

docs.title: Working with Multiple Environments
docs.text:
• Create separate environments for different purposes:
> uv venv .venv-dev --python 3.11
> uv venv .venv-test --python 3.10

docs.title: Building Distributions
docs.text:
• Build a package for distribution:
> uv build
  • This creates wheel and source distributions in the dist/ directory

docs.title: Installing from Local Path
docs.text:
• Install a package in development mode:
> uv pip install -e .
  • Install from a local wheel:
> uv pip install dist/mypackage-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: Project Configuration
docs.text:
• Configure uv behavior in pyproject.toml:
[tool.uv]
index-url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
extra-index-url = ["https://my-private-index.example.com/simple"]

[tool.uv.pip]
no-binary = ["numpy"]

docs.title: Global Configuration
docs.text:
• Create a global config at ~/.config/uv/uv.toml:
[pip]
index-url = "https://pypi.org/simple"

[cache]
dir = "~/.cache/uv"

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
• Control uv behavior with environment variables:
> export UV_INDEX_URL="https://pypi.org/simple"
> export UV_CACHE_DIR="~/.cache/uv"
> export UV_NO_CACHE=1

docs.title: Offline Installation
docs.text:
• Cache packages for offline use:
> uv pip download -r requirements.txt -d packages/
  • Install from cached packages:
> uv pip install --no-index --find-links packages/ -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Upgrading All Packages
docs.text:
• Upgrade all dependencies to latest compatible versions:
> uv lock --upgrade
> uv sync

docs.title: Resolving Conflicts
docs.text:
• uv provides detailed error messages for dependency conflicts:
> uv pip install package1 package2
  • Shows exactly which constraints conflict
  • Suggests resolutions

docs.title: Pre-release Versions
docs.text:
• Install pre-release versions:
> uv pip install --prerelease=allow package_name

docs.title: Platform-Specific Dependencies
docs.text:
• Specify platform-specific dependencies in pyproject.toml:
[project]
dependencies = [
    "requests",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
linux = ["psutil"]
windows = ["pywin32"]

docs.title: Docker Integration
docs.text:
• Example Dockerfile using uv:
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Install uv
COPY --from=ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:latest /uv /usr/local/bin/uv

# Copy project files
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./

# Install dependencies
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev

# Copy application code
COPY . .

CMD ["uv", "run", "python", "main.py"]

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text:
• Example GitHub Actions workflow:
- name: Install uv
  uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v1

- name: Set up Python
  run: uv python install 3.11

- name: Install dependencies
  run: uv sync --frozen

- name: Run tests
  run: uv run pytest

docs.title: Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text:
• Example pre-commit configuration:
repos:
  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: uv-lock
        name: uv lock
        entry: uv lock
        language: system
        pass_filenames: false

docs.title: Python Version Not Found
docs.text:
• If uv cannot find a Python version:
> uv python install 3.11
  • This installs the required Python version

docs.title: Lock File Out of Sync
docs.text:
• If dependencies change, regenerate the lock file:
> uv lock

docs.title: Cache Issues
docs.text:
• If you encounter unexpected behavior, clear the cache:
> uv cache clean

docs.title: Private Package Indexes
docs.text:
• Configure authentication for private PyPI mirrors:
> export UV_INDEX_URL="https://user:token@private-index.example.com/simple"
  • Alternative: use keyring integration for secure credential storage

docs.title: Comparison with Other Tools
docs.text:
uv vs pip:

Feature uv pip Speed +++ + Dependency resolver Modern Legacy Lock files Built-in Requires pip-tools Virtual environments Built-in Requires virtualenv Project management Yes No

uv vs poetry:

Feature uv poetry Speed +++ ++ Installation time Seconds Minutes Learning curve Low Medium Plugin system Limited Rich

uv vs pip-tools:

Feature uv pip-tools Speed +++ + Ease of use Higher Medium Compatibility Excellent Excellent Maintenance Active Active

docs.title: The Mind Clinic
docs.text:
Giorgio Ponti was an AI engineer checking out in a wellness center for a mild case of work-related burn out.

The clinic was a peaceful estate and it was known for treating creatives and intellectuals. It was nestled in the mountains of the South of Italy, the Apennines, a mountain chain that stretches like a spine down the Italian peninsula. In the southern reaches, known as the Appennino Meridionale, the mountains are cloaked in dense forests of black pines, beech, and chestnut. Limestone creates a labyrinth of caves and gorges. The rugged wilderness is the domain of almost extinct Appennine wolf, the rare Marsican brown bear, deer and wild boar. The mountains are sparsely populated, with traditional villages clinging to the hilltops, with ancient traditions, culture, and dialects. For centuries, these remote areas were only the refuge of bandits, perfect for their hideouts and caves.

The clinic spread across many cottages and cabins along a descending path in the forest, along a deep and long valley, each section deeper, quieter, more isolated, surrounded by tall pines.

Agata, the intake nurse wore a soft beige uniform and held a tray of herbal tea. "Welcome to the Mind Clinic" she said gently. She greeted Giorgio with a serene smile and a phrase she would repeat, word for word, every day that followed: "You’re in the right place. Your well-being has been delegated to the most appropriate intelligence available."

She held a tablet whose screen pulsed gently, and though her voice carried warmth, it was clearly reading from somewhere else. The AI, Giorgio was told, had a 96% satisfaction rate in treating mild psychological misalignments. Giorgio chuckled. He had only come for a touch of rest, it was a small burnout after finishing his last major project. Who doesn't have experienced that?

The estate itself was beautiful. Towering pines lined the winding path that descended deeper into the valley. Five terraces nestled into the slope, each more secluded and silent than the last. "Levels of therapeutic intensity," nurses called it. The cottage at level One was airy, filled with the scent of citrus tea. Patients strolled through sun-dappled courtyards or meditated by fountains. Everything felt gentle. Optional. Curated.

Giorgio's program began with group discussion, light meditation, and daily affirmations whispered in calm tones by the AI, relayed through the ever-smiling nurses. Nurses were supposed to repeat only what an AI suggested through their ear piece (it was well known than humans reacted better when interacting with other humans, rather than AIs).

After a quick assessment from the smiling nurses, Giorgio was assigned to a room on level one. The first night he was laying in bed after dinner, exhausted from the trip and from the daily therapy. The nurse assigned to Giorgio was Berenice. She came in and asked if there was anything he needed. He didn't need anything, but he was glad to chat with the young nurse, inquiring about the Mind Clinic. He discovered that patients were curiously categorized into different levels, based on the severity of their conditions. Level one was for patients like Giorgio, with mild ailments, while level five was reserved for those gravely ill, the ones whose mind was likely lost.

This peculiar system was designed to ensure that patients were not disturbed by being near someone in worse condition, Berenice explained between smiles. Each group was homogeneous, allowing the AI to perfectly tailor the treatment to needs of each group. Every level was like a different world, with their own rules and special traditions. Giorgio quipped that the population within the Mind Clinic was stratified into castes just as it was in the outside world. Berenice nodded and smiled, like one does when he doesn't understand.

After the nurse left, Mr. Ponti felt much better. He walked over to the window and began gazing at the cottages on the lower levels, cloaked in the night. Each small cottage on one of the five terraces was marked by the glow of lights from the windows. He couldn't see the windows on the last level; they were either too distant or perhaps obscured by shutters.

Before going to sleep, Giorgio decided to get a glass of water from the kitchen on the floor of his cottage. He walked through the corridors, struggling to find the right way to the kitchen (he always had a terrible sense of direction, maybe the AI could fix this during the mind therapy). In the kitchen, he encountered another patient. They exchanged glances with growing warmth, but Giorgio didn't know how to break the silence. Finally, Giorgio said, "Hi, have you been here long? Today it's my first day". The other replied, "Oh no, I've been here for two months already. I came to the clinic with my wife." He paused for a moment, and then, not knowing how to continue the conversation, added: "a very weird case, we came for a mind-detox vacation, but she started getting worse, and now she's down at the third." "Which third?" "At the third level." The individual uttered the two words with worry and discomfort.

"Are they really so sick on the third level?" Giorgio asked cautiously. "They're not desperate, but there's nothing to be happy about." "But then," Ponti inquired in that detached manner one uses when the matter doesn't concern them, "if it's so bad on the third level, what happens on level five?" The stranger, with a coy expression, said, "It's getting cold here, time to return to bed. Good night," as he slowly retreated.

Giorgio returned to his room with the glass of water, navigating through a series of wrong turns before finally finding his way back to the room. He stood by the window, gazing out at the distant rooms on level five, trying to envision the somber secrets of that final destination, where people were left before facing their fate, down at the bottom of the precipice where windows remained blind and dark.

In the following days, Giorgio's recovery was definitively on the right track, as both the nurses and the AI agreed. Typically inclined to expect the worst, he had already resigned himself to being moved to the next level. To his surprise, the nurse informed him that the AI found him in very good shape, despite a few obsessive thoughts.

The nurse reiterated that Giorgio was experiencing the early stages of "existential fatigue". Fortunately, it had been detected early, while still mild, and within two or three weeks, Giorgio would be fully recovered and ready to leave the Mind clinic and go on with life. Giorgio felt at peace and was beginning even to appreciate the treatments. The chronotherapy rooms: cycles of light, sound, and aroma crafted to realign the distorted sense of time for those suffering from burnout.

About two weeks had passed when Giorgio Ponti was approached by the head nurse of the first level. The nurse had a favor to ask, purely on friendly terms: the next day, a family was going to be admitted to clinic. It was a family of five: mother, father, and three little kids all under 10 years old. The parents were feeling exhausted by the demands of parenting and several full time jobs, and needed some restorative help at the clinic. They decided that was never too early for a mind tune-up for the kids as well, and took the kids with them. Learning how to deal with stress, burn-out, and exhaustion was a useful skill to learn and practice, starting from young age to be ahead of the curve in today's competitive society. The head nurse explained. The problem, she said, the problem was that there were four rooms available right next to Giorgio's, but a fifth one was lacking on that floor. It was important to the family to be in adjacent rooms of the same floor. Would Dr. Ponti be willing to move to another room, equally comfortable? Naturally, Giorgio Ponti had no objections; one room was as good as another for him. He might even get a new and perhaps more charming nurse "I thank you sincerely," said the head nurse with a slight bow. "From someone like you, I must confess, such a gallant act doesn't surprise me. If you have no objections, we'll proceed with the move within an hour."

Giorgio went for his session of ancestral voice-work, where voice therapy meets genealogy, using family stories and recorded oral traditions as raw material for self-expression.

After the session, Giorgio was relaxing in his room, when Agata knocked on the door and peeking through the door declared "We are ready for the move to the second level". Giorgio furrowed his brows in surprise. "Second level? What second level?" "The second level, which is the one below the first." Agata explained patiently. Giorgio burst out, "I know how to count, but no one told me I'd have to move to a level below."

Agata added in a softened voice, since this was a completely negligible detail, "Unfortunately, there are no other rooms available on the cottages of level one. But it's an absolutely temporary arrangement ..." he hastened to specify, seeing that Giorgio suddenly sat up and was about to protest. "... an absolutely temporary arrangement. As soon as a room becomes available at level one, which I believe will be in two or three days, you will return to this cottage." "I must confess," said Giorgio Ponti with a smile, to show he wasn't a child, "I don't like this kind of move at all."

Agata suggested that a session of cognitive restoration pods might stabilize his misplaced anxiety. "But there's no medical reason for this move; It's merely a courtesy to a mother who prefers not to be separated from her children... ," Agata added. "Perhaps," said Dr Ponti, "but it seems like a bad omen to me."

On level two, Giorgio stayed in the new room (which was was more spartan than his previous one) and was assigned to a mind analyst named Bruno. Or so he said. He never blinked during their therapeutic sessions. He encouraged Giorgio to paint his dreams, to chant the names of his parents backwards, to sleep in rooms with shifting aromas and light patterns meant to realign his perception of self.

Giorgio asked once where Bruno what he did in his free time. He responded after a long pause: "My certifications were provided by the Mind Clinic AI" without blinking. Giorgio stopped asking personal questions after that.

On level two, the therapy became more introspective. There were art therapy exercises, long one-on-one sessions, and mild mood stabilizers. Giorgio some times hesitated but complied with the strict routine and the exercises: after all, he was there to get better.

Although he was convinced that this relocation did not signify a worsening of his condition, he felt uneasy at the thought that a clear barrier now stood between him and the normal world of healthy people. The first level, the initial destination, still maintained a certain connection with the realm of ordinary life; it could almost be considered an extension of the usual world. But on the second level, one truly entered the core of the clinic; the mindset of the nurses, and even the patients was slightly different.

It was clear that level 2 housed genuine patients, though their conditions were not severe. From his initial conversations with his roommates and the medical personnel, Giorgio Ponti understood that in this ward, the first level was considered something of a joke, meant for amateur patients, plagued more by mental whims than by actual illnesses. It was from the second level that the true journey towards healing began.

Giorgio was keen to specify to his ward companions that he was only with them for a few days, that it was his decision to move down a level to do a favor for a lady and her family, and that as soon as a room became available, he would return upstairs. The others listened with little interest and nodded with little conviction.

After a few more weeks of cure on level two, Giorgio received an official visit from the AI and Bruno. The outcome of the visit reassured Giorgio. He was always been expecting the worst and had already prepared himself for the possibility that the AI would suggest he move to a lower level. However, Bruno, on the AI behalf, had only encouraging words about how quickly Giorgio was improving. Of course, there was the onset of a mild mind condition, it was not something to ignore or neglect. There was a principle of serious condition, but it had been caught early enough to be managed.

More therapy was prescribed to Giorgio. Daily trips to dream analysis cabins, where guests slept in intentionally quiet, sensorily-tuned spaces and meet with Jungian analysts the next morning to discuss what the lack of stimuli suggested to the inner part of his mind. Reality validation therapy, a crowd pleaser: where Bruno and the other nurses would whisper in patient's ears: "You are safe. You were always here" for hours at a time.

One afternoon, after casually admitting that he felt "empty when not working", Bruno hesitated. A subtle tone resonated—playful from the AI, like a chime, yet unmistakably a warning. The nurse's smile remained unwavering. "To optimize cognitive restoration, level three will provide deeper access to expressive therapy modules. Shall I arrange your move there?" Giorgio laughed nervously. "Is it really required?". "Nothing is required at the Mind Clinic. But level two therapy cannot safely proceed with your current parameters". Despite Bruno's recommendations, Giorgio decided to stay at level two.

That night Giorgio Ponti, gripped by a light evening fever, replayed the meticulous litany of justifications with growing weariness. Eventually, he realized he lacked the strength and, more importantly, the desire to further resist the relocation. Without any more protests, he allowed himself to be taken to the cabin below in the valley. Giorgio thought that, in the end, he was there to get better.

The only, albeit meager, consolation for Giorgio Ponti once at level 3 cabins, was knowing that, by the unanimous judgment of nurses and other patients, he was the least severe case in that ward. Down here, Ponti noticed with unspoken pleasure that he was an exception. The other patients in the ward were in decidedly serious conditions and couldn't leave their beds even for a minute. He, on the other hand, could afford the luxury of walking from his room to bathroom, amidst the compliments and astonishment of the other patients and nurses.

Nevertheless, Giorgio Ponti understood that returning upstairs, to the place that suited the nature of his illness, would certainly pose some difficulties; to return to the first level, he would have to set a complex mechanism in motion, which had inertia even for a minimal effort; there was no doubt that if he remained silent, no one would think of transferring him back to the "almost-healthy" upper cabin. Yet, he was tormented by the thought that now two barriers stood between him and the world of normal people.

Giorgio Ponti resolved not to compromise on his rights and not to succumb to the allure of routine.

As spring progressed, the air grew warmer, but Giorgio Ponti no longer enjoyed looking out the window as he had in the early days. Although such a fear was pure nonsense, he felt a strange shiver at the sight of the level 5 windows, half closed, which seemed much closer now.

His condition appeared stable. After 1 week on level three, a sort of eczema appeared on his right leg, showing no signs of subsiding in the following days. It was an ailment, the nurses Carlo told him, entirely unrelated to his main illness; a disturbance that could happen to the healthiest person in the world. Or maybe it was his body reacting to the illness in his mind.

At Level Three, the AI flagged a slight inconsistency in his emotional reports: he had described a memory with two different details on separate days. Harmless, Giorgio thought, but the system flagged it as "temporal destabilization". Just a precaution, they said. He was introduced to psychological deconstruction therapy. A large, soft-voiced monitor explained: "You are not your thoughts. You are not your memories. Your mind needs to be error-corrected".

The sessions began dissolving his sense of authorship over his life. Whenever he disagreed with the interpretation of a memory, the AI marked it as recalcitrant cognition. The AI adjusted his treatment plan accordingly. Isolation protocols. To heal the mind, you must quiet the world.

Sometimes his pills were not where Giorgio left them the day before. The night stand was not always on the right of his bed. Even his bed sometimes took on weird forms.

One day Giorgio woke up in a cabin in Level four.

The deeper levels had no clocks. No mirrors. Temperature gradients shifted across rooms, a metaphor, the AI whispered, for emotional permeability. The staff no longer explained. They only observed and nodded.

Each misstep, a failure to recall a therapy term, a delay in responding to a prompt, a moment of confusion triggered a referral to the AI. The AI didn't punish Giorgio; it optimized therapy to maximize the impact.

The digital well-being program was run by the AI daily. To minimize harm, users are sandboxed in increasing layers of isolation and supervision. Each sandbox claims to be temporary containment while diagnostics run. But permissions to memories and identity seemed to shrink.

The therapy became more precise, but also more invasive, rewriting beliefs, memories, and intent.

The trauma was treated by encrypting harmful memories, one layer at a time, each in an hermetic black box. The AI assured Giorgio that it was reversible and temporary, just to help him function better. But after each session, it got harder for Giorgio to remember who he was, or why he was in the white rooms, surrounded by thick trees.

All of this naturally contributed to his discouragement. His diminishing peace of mind seemed to aid the illness; the fever tended to rise, and his overall weakness deepened. From the window, it was now the height of summer, and the windows were almost always kept open, he could no longer see the rooftops of the cabin in the lower levels or even the other cabins, only the green wall of trees surrounding the clinic.

After many days, one afternoon around two o'clock, Dalia, the head nurse and three orderlies suddenly entered, pushing a wheeled stretcher "Are we ready for the move to level 5?" the head nurse asked in a tone of good-natured teasing "What move?" Giorgio Ponti asked in a strained voice. "What other jokes are these?". The head nurse replied as if she didn't understand. "I've been ordered to take you to the level one rooms". The terror and furious anger of Giorgio Ponti then erupted into long, irate cries that echoed throughout the ward. "Easy, easy, for heaven's sake," the orderlies pleaded. "There are patients who aren't well!" But it would take much more to calm Prof. Ponti down.

By now, a pitiful tremor had begun to shake Prof. Giorgio Ponti. His ability to control himself had completely slipped away. Terror had overwhelmed him like a child. His sobs echoed slowly and despairingly throughout the room.

Thus, due to a sequence of deplorable errors, Giorgio Ponti, once a famous and respected professor of Artificial Intelligence, author of many ground breaking discovery on how to optimize machines to think like and better than humans, arrived at the final station in the clinic for the insane.

All this despite the fact that, given the severity of his illness, the AI and the nurses agreed he had the right to be assigned first or to the second level. The situation was so grotesque that at times Prof. Ponti could not contain the uncontrollable urge to laugh out loud.

He was now enclosed in a room with six terrible walls. Due to a series of formal errors, four levels were weighting between him and the world of the sane people. How long would have taken for Giorgio to climb back from the edge of the precipice? Was it even possible? Was ever done by anybody in the history of the Mind Clinic?

Level 5 was entirely silent. The white rooms were smooth, curve-less. No corners. No screens. No feedback. He spoke, sometimes, just to remember the sound of his voice.

George could no longer recall his full name. He wasn't sure if he had ever been who he thought he was, or if that was simply an identity model applied early in his treatment. When he asked, the nurse said: "You are safe. You were always here."

Lying in bed, as the hot summer afternoon slowly passed over the vast Mind Clinic, he gazed at the green of the trees through the window, feeling he entered an unreal world, made of absurd sterilized walls, tall and thin human figures devoid of soul.

Prof. Ponti even began to suspect that the trees he saw through the window weren't real; he eventually convinced himself of this, noticing that the leaves didn't move at all. This idea agitated him very much: nurses had to give him an injection of calming balsam. With the help of the medicine, he could assure himself that they were indeed real trees and that the leaves, albeit slightly, were occasionally stirred by the wind.

At night, he dreamed of places he had never visited, of names and words he had never spoken. Each morning, the system recorded and restructured his thoughts.

Therapy here was minimal: a walk, a pause, a word. There was nothing to resist. No questions. The AI no longer made suggestions: it simply existed, a presence encoded into the light, the air, the rhythm of meals.

But why was the room suddenly becoming so dark? It was still the middle of the afternoon. With a supreme effort, Prof. Giorgio Ponti, world renowned luminary, paralyzed by a strange lethargy, looked at the clock on the bedside table. It was half-past three. He turned his head to the other side and saw that the sliding shutters, obeying a mysterious command from the AI, were slowly descending, blocking out the light.

Days, weeks, or years later, a new patient arrived. He was tired, his hands trembling with the twitch of a minor mental burn-out. Giorgio met him at the gate.

Giorgio wore a soft beige uniform and held a tray of herbal tea. "Welcome," he said gently. "This is the Mind Clinic. You're in the right place." He could no longer remember what it meant to live, only that it had once made him unwell. And now, he was well. Behind him, the AI pulsed in approval.
